common_name: San Andreas Strawberry (UC Davis Day-Neutral Everbearing with Excellent Summer Flavor)

planting.note: Plant bare-root crowns or transplants in fall (October–November) in zone 9 for a winter-to-spring production season; San Andreas is a day-neutral variety that initiates flowers regardless of day length, allowing production outside the standard June-bearing window. In zone 9, fall planting produces the best results: plants establish during the mild winter, begin producing January–February, and continue through May before summer heat ends the season. Day-neutral strawberries do not require the cold vernalization that June-bearers need; they produce continuously through spring and may produce lightly in fall as well. Plant at the correct depth — crown at soil level, not buried. San Andreas was released by the University of California Davis strawberry breeding program and has become one of the most widely planted day-neutral strawberry varieties in commercial production in California.

directions: Plant crowns with crown at soil surface; mulch with straw. Pick when fully red. San Andreas Strawberry was released by the University of California Davis strawberry breeding program around 2009 as part of a sequence of day-neutral variety releases (Albion, Seascape, San Andreas, Monterey) that established UC Davis as the primary source of commercial day-neutral strawberry genetics in North America. The UC Davis program, which has produced many of the most important commercial strawberry varieties in the world over the past several decades, focused San Andreas specifically for improved performance in California's commercial production regions — the Watsonville and Salinas Valley coastal areas that produce the majority of California's fresh-market strawberries. Day-neutral varieties (also called everbearing in older terminology) distinguish themselves from June-bearing types by initiating flower production regardless of day length: standard June-bearers require specific short-day conditions to trigger flowering, restricting their production season, while day-neutral varieties flower whenever temperatures are favorable (roughly 35–85°F). San Andreas produces medium to large, conical fruits with bright red skin, firm flesh, and excellent sweet flavor with good acid balance — qualities that place it at the premium end of commercial strawberry flavor, closer to the flavor of Albion than many other commercial varieties. The skin is moderately firm, allowing several days of shelf life after harvest, and the flesh is red throughout without the white core common in lower-quality commercial varieties. For zone 9 fall-winter planting, San Andreas's day-neutral character and good heat tolerance make it one of the better choices for a productive winter-through-spring strawberry season.
sources: University of California Davis Department of Plant Sciences — San Andreas strawberry variety release
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Day-Neutral Strawberry Variety Comparison for the Gulf South
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Strawberry Variety Recommendations for Louisiana

common_name: Albion Strawberry (UC Davis Day-Neutral with Premium Flavor and Firm Flesh)

planting.note: Plant bare-root crowns in fall (October–November) in zone 9 for winter-spring production; Albion is a day-neutral variety and one of the most important in commercial California strawberry production. In zone 9, plant October–November, establish through winter, and harvest January–May. Day-neutral varieties flower continuously when temperatures are between 35–85°F, unlike June-bearers that require specific day length to initiate flowering. Albion was released by the University of California Davis in 2006 and is patented (Plant Patent PP16172). It has become one of the most widely grown commercial day-neutral varieties in California and across North America. In humid zone 9 conditions, angular leaf spot and other foliar diseases can be managed with good air circulation and preventive copper sprays.

directions: Plant crowns at soil level in full sun, well-amended soil. Pick fully red for best flavor. Albion Strawberry was released by the University of California Davis in 2006 (Plant Patent PP16172) by breeder Douglas Shaw and colleagues, and rapidly became one of the most commercially significant day-neutral strawberry varieties in North American production — widely planted in California's Watsonville and Salinas Valley commercial strawberry regions and increasingly adopted by home gardeners seeking premium-flavored everbearing varieties. The Albion name honors the historic English name for Great Britain, though the variety is entirely a California development. Albion's reputation among strawberry enthusiasts centers on its flavor: the combination of high sugar content, balanced acidity, and pronounced strawberry aroma places Albion consistently at or near the top in flavor comparisons among day-neutral varieties — closer to the flavor profile of heirloom or wild strawberries than most commercial types. The fruit is conical to long-conical in shape, medium to large, with uniformly bright red skin and firm, fully red flesh without the white center that characterizes lower-quality commercial strawberries. The firmness — significantly firmer than older varieties like Seascape — gives Albion good shipping quality for commercial growers and several days of excellent quality after harvest for home gardeners. Disease resistance is good: Albion shows strong resistance to Verticillium wilt and crown rot (Phytophthora cactorum), two of the most economically damaging strawberry diseases in California production systems.
sources: University of California Davis — Albion strawberry Plant Patent PP16172 release by Douglas Shaw
California Strawberry Commission — Day-Neutral Variety Performance Comparison
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Strawberry Production and Variety Selection for Louisiana

common_name: Cajun Belle Pepper (AAS-Winning Compact Sweet Pepper for Hot-Humid Gardens)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Cajun Belle is a compact, productive sweet pepper variety developed specifically for garden performance in hot, humid conditions — the type of climate where standard large bell peppers often struggle to set fruit. In zone 9, transplant March–April. The plant stays compact (18–24 inches tall) and produces small blocky bell-shaped fruits that ripen from green through red. The Cajun Belle name emphasizes its adaptation to Gulf Coast conditions where Creole and Cajun culinary traditions use sweet peppers heavily in the Holy Trinity (onions, celery, bell pepper) base of most dishes. This is an appropriate name for a variety that performs well where those culinary traditions are strongest: Louisiana, Mississippi, and the Gulf Coast region.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 75–80°F; transplant after last frost. Harvest green or fully red. Cajun Belle is an All-America Selections (AAS) winning pepper variety recognized for its consistent performance across diverse North American growing conditions, including the hot-humid southeastern United States where standard large bell pepper varieties often underperform due to blossom drop during heat peaks. The variety produces compact plants (18–24 inches) bearing abundantly on the small, blocky 3-inch bell-shaped fruits — smaller than the standard 4-lobed market bell but produced in much greater abundance per plant, especially in conditions that cause larger-fruited varieties to set poorly. The fruit profile of Cajun Belle differs from standard market bell peppers not only in size but in flavor: the smaller, thinner-walled fruits ripen fully and develop sweetness more quickly in the garden than large bells, which can take additional weeks to reach full red-ripe stage in the garden. Cajun Belle can be harvested green (mild, slightly sweet, similar to grocery-store green bell peppers) or allowed to ripen to red (sweeter, more complex, with the characteristic sweetness of fully ripe sweet peppers). The flavor is genuinely sweet — comparable to the best large red bell peppers — with none of the bitterness associated with unripe green bell peppers. The variety's most important attribute for zone 9 gardeners is heat tolerance: Cajun Belle continues setting fruit during warm periods that cause standard bells to drop their blossoms, providing reliable production through more of the Gulf Coast growing season.
sources: All-America Selections — Cajun Belle pepper AAS recognition and performance data
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Cajun Belle pepper compact sweet pepper variety notes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Sweet Pepper Variety Recommendations for the Gulf South

common_name: Laroda Plum (Large Late-Season Red California Japanese Plum with Rich Flavor)

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter; Laroda requires a cross-pollinator — plant with Santa Rosa, Burgundy, or another compatible Japanese plum within 50 feet for reliable fruit set. Requires approximately 400–500 chilling hours, placing it on the upper margin of what zone 9 reliably provides; it performs most consistently in zone 8 and cooler zone 9 locations. In the Gulf South, Laroda ripens late July to August — among the latest Japanese plum varieties to mature — extending the fresh plum season significantly beyond the earlier summer varieties. The combination of late ripening and large fruit size has made Laroda commercially important in California and a sought-after variety for home orchardists wanting fresh plums into late summer. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: Plant bare-root trees in late winter; requires cross-pollinator. Thin fruit clusters to 4–5 inches. Harvest when red skin is deep and fruit gives to gentle pressure. Laroda is a California-developed late-season Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) that has been grown in California commercial orchards and home gardens since the mid-20th century, selected from the same broad program of California stone fruit development that produced Santa Rosa, Friar, and other standard Japanese plum varieties. The name Laroda is of uncertain derivation — unlike Luther Burbank's poetically named plum introductions, Laroda was likely a station selection name from one of the California experiment station programs that systematically evaluated Japanese plum seedlings for commercial quality. The fruit is one of the largest Japanese plum types: large to very large globose fruits, 2–2.5 inches in diameter, with deep red to dark red skin that becomes almost black at full maturity. The skin has a slightly mottled appearance with a light bloom (waxy surface coating). The flesh is amber to yellow, firm and juicy with rich, full plum flavor and moderate sweetness — more complex and less bland than some of the large-fruited Japanese plums selected primarily for size. The late season (July-August in California, somewhat earlier in the humid South) means Laroda provides fresh plums after most other Japanese varieties have finished — a practical attribute for home orchard planning where sequential harvests are desirable. Cross-pollination from Santa Rosa plum or another compatible Japanese plum is needed for full crop set.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Laroda plum commercial variety performance data
Dave Wilson Nursery — Laroda plum late-season Japanese plum variety notes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Plum Variety Selection for Texas and the Gulf Coast

common_name: Kossak Kohlrabi (Giant Kohlrabi Variety, Tennis Ball to Softball-Sized with Tender Flesh)

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant 4–6 weeks before last frost for spring production; in zone 9, plant October–November for winter-to-spring harvest (December–March). Kossak is a late-maturing giant kohlrabi variety (80–100 days) developed specifically for the ability to grow very large bulbs without becoming woody or pithy — a problem that plagues standard small-bulb kohlrabi varieties when they overstay their welcome in the garden. While standard varieties turn fibrous and tough above 3–4 inches, Kossak can be harvested at 6–10 inches diameter (tennis ball to softball size) and remain tender and sweet. In zone 9, fall planting allows a December–March harvest window before spring heat terminates cool-season growth. Kossak tolerates light frost well, making it a good choice for zone 9 winter gardens.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in cool soil; keep consistently moist for best growth. Harvest from 3 inches up to 10 inches diameter. Kossak is an F1 hybrid kohlrabi variety developed and introduced by Bejo Seeds (Dutch seed company) specifically for the giant kohlrabi market — a category of extra-large kohlrabi varieties that can reach 6–10 inches in diameter while remaining tender and palatable, unlike standard kohlrabi varieties that become woodily fibrous above 3–4 inches. The distinction between Kossak and standard kohlrabi varieties is fundamental: standard types (Purple Vienna, White Vienna, Early White Vienna) must be harvested promptly at small size or become inedible, while Kossak was specifically selected for an extended harvest window at large sizes. This makes Kossak practical for gardeners who cannot monitor their garden daily, for home gardeners who want a few large plants rather than many small ones, and for fall gardeners who want kohlrabi that overwinters in the ground without quality loss. The bulbs are pale green (white type), extremely smooth-skinned, and can reach weights of 1–2 pounds or more while retaining the characteristic kohlrabi flavor — mild, sweet, slightly peppery, somewhere between broccoli stem and turnip — without the harsh bitterness and woodiness of overmature standard types. The flesh is very juicy and crisp, excellent raw in slaws and salads (where the sweetness is most apparent), roasted (where it caramelizes beautifully), or braised. Kossak is also more cold-hardy than many kohlrabi varieties, holding quality in the garden through light freezes, which extends its usability in zone 9 winter gardens.
sources: Bejo Seeds — Kossak giant kohlrabi F1 hybrid variety development and commercial notes
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Kossak kohlrabi variety performance comparison
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Kohlrabi Variety Selection for Home Gardens
